data.title: ICF: Providing Passive Exposure To An Industry Diversified Basket Of REITs
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman, co-produced by Stanford Chemist

The iShares Select U.S. REIT ETF (ICF) provides investors with passive exposure to a broad basket of real estate investment trust holdings. The index is based on the Cohen & Steers Realty Majors Index—Cohen & Steers (CNS) is a well-known investment manager with a specific focus on real estate investments.

With that said, and as we have previously noted, the index that's constructed hasn't delivered a significant amount of alpha relative to the other real estate-focused ETFs available for investors. In fact, over the longer term, it has notably fallen short relative to the other choices available to investors.

ICF Basics

  • Dividend Frequency: Quarterly
  • Dividend Yield: 2.55% (SEC yield 2.68%)
  • Expense Ratio: 0.32%
  • Leverage: N/A
  • Managed Assets: $1.881 billion
  • Structure: Passive ETF

ICF's investment objective is to "track the investment results of an index composed of U.S. real estate investment trusts." Providing exposure to "large real estate companies that are dominant in their respective property sectors."

We covered a deeper outline in our initial coverage of this fund, and the full report can be read, but we'll highlight the major points.

  • First, and probably it goes without saying, it has to be a REIT.
  • The market cap has to be over $500 million.
  • Average trading volume needs to be at least 600k shares for six months.
  • Then the active part of the CNS comes in as they select the 30 they believe "best represent the Realty Majors."

After that, they rank holdings by market cap, but no position can be over an 8% weighting. The fund rebalances on the third Friday of February, May, August, and November.

This fund pays a quarterly distribution. Similar to most ETFs, it is based on what it receives in dividends paid from the underlying holdings, and that is passed on to investors. That means the payout changes every quarter, and as we've seen for the last several years now, that can be quite drastic differences from quarter to quarter.

Performance Comparison

Since our last update, the fund has delivered slightly positive total returns—but certainly nothing to boast about, as it significantly underperformed the S&P 500 Index.

Looking at more appropriate direct comparisons in the REIT space and we see that again, the fund really can't boast about anything.

Over the longer term, ICF has underperformed the State Street Real Estate Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLRE) by a wide margin. The fund has also fallen quite short relative to the other real estate-focused iShares offering, the iShares US Real Estate ETF (IYR). Against the Vanguard Real Estate Index Fund ETF (VNQ), it has been able to put up a bit more respectable numbers.

In looking back at the last five years, we have an interesting period of time that was particularly rough on REITs, the very aggressive interest rate hikes we saw from the Fed, and the rising long-term risk-free yields. Both of which negatively impact REITs.

First, higher yields mean safer alternatives can start to look better as a source of income for investors, who can then offload their riskier REIT holdings.

Second, REITs utilize a high level of leverage; rising rates mean their borrowing costs will be subject to higher interest rates as well.

With that, a look at the last five years for all of these ETFs shows relatively low returns, but interestingly, ICF does start to look more respectable.

For passively managed index-tracking ETFs, it can sometimes come down to something as simple as higher or lower expense ratios. For ICF, the expense ratio comes in at 32 basis points; XLRE is at a razor-thin 8 bps, with VNQ at 13 bps and IYR at 38 bps. So that is playing some of a factor, but clearly isn't the only thing going on here for the fund over the longer term, as the underperformance was quite significant.

ICF Portfolio Comparison

The fund is fairly diversified across different REITs focused on various industries. However, the fund is also fairly concentrated in several of the top holdings, which can take some of the benefits of being truly diversified away.

Welltower (WELL), Prologis REIT (PLD) and American Tower REIT (AMT) all take commanding weightings at over 8%, resulting in those three positions alone being ~25% of the fund's total investments.

Still, each does belong to a very distinct underlying industry that is very different from the other: healthcare, industrial, and cell tower, respectively.

With that, given the market-cap-weighted ranking that is popular among many of the passively managed index funds, we will see some significant overlap with its peers.

In the case of ICF vs. XLRE, we have 83% overlap with 25 holdings. ICF holdings only 30 positions, with XLRE at 31.

In looking toward the top holdings, we can see exactly what we expected to see. WELL, PLD and AMT are the largest positions, followed by Equinix (EQIX) and Simon Property Group (SPG).

With ICF vs. VNQ, we see the percentage overlap being quite a bit lower. However, VNQ holdings 160 positions—which actually results in holding all 30 of the same holdings of ICF. Therefore, the actual overlap is reduced due to the sheer number of other holdings.

Despite the meaningfully greater number of holdings, looking at the top holdings, we see once again significant overlap. The weightings for WELL and PLD are almost identical.

In the case of ICF and IYR, we similarly see that the percentage overlap is lower when compared to XLRE, but again, it is due to the higher number of holdings for IYR at 61 holdings.

For the top holdings, we can see a slight difference here, in that SPG is a larger holding than AMT for IYR. However, that's quite a minor difference.

With that, despite the longer-term showing ICF as a weaker performer, the significant overlap that we've seen from the holdings could suggest that we'll see much closer performance at this time. That's consistent with what we've been seeing for the last five years, so that looks set to continue.

Conclusion

Over the long term, ICF has underperformed its passively managed ETF peers, which is disappointing to see from an index modeled by CNS, a niche asset manager in the real estate space. However, over the last five years, during a period that was overall having quite significant headwinds for REITs, the fund has started to perform much more respectably. Given the overlap of these peer funds today, similar performance would be expected to continue as well. That doesn't give ICF too much of an edge, but with a lower rate environment as the Fed continues its cutting cycle, that should bode well for REITs more broadly.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Zscaler Has Fallen But The Neighborhood Is On Sale
data.description:
Sometimes patience can be the hardest thing in investing. Zscaler (ZS) has pulled back significantly in the past quarter. Those who avoided the stock during the summer months may be viewing the recent weakness as a potential buying opportunity. I urge further patience, as one can find no shortage of even cheaper names in the software space. The stock may look more buyable than earlier in the year, but it is a buyer’s market in the software space. I reiterate my neutral rating for the stock.

ZS Stock Price

I last covered ZS in October, where I cautioned on the valuation. The stock has fallen 23% since.

The valuation contraction has helped the valuation proposition, but I should note the greater slide seen in the broader software space.

ZS Stock Key Metrics

ZS is a cybersecurity enterprise software company. It is disrupting the traditional firewall product with its “zero trust” protocol. The easy way to think about it is that whereas legacy firewalls protect access to the network, ZS allows its customers to customize security access on an app-by-app basis. This is far superior and is what’s needed in the cloud economy.

The company has also been a beneficiary of the rise of AI, as it has introduced products to secure access to AI. I expect these products to gain more traction as AI further makes its way through society.

In the most recent quarter, the company generated 26% YoY revenue growth to $788 million, crushing guidance of between $772 million to $774 million.

The generated $172 million in non-GAAP operating profits, exceeding guidance of between $166 million to $168 million and representing around 100 bps of margin expansion.

The company did not make progress towards GAAP profitability, but I am not concerned, as this is the kind of business in which I can see management ramping up margins if and when they decide to do so.

ZS ended the quarter with $3.3 billion of cash and investments versus $1.7 billion in convertible notes, representing a strong net cash balance sheet. I note that using convertible notes has the benefit of lower interest expenses.

Looking ahead, management guided the second quarter to see around 23% YoY revenue growth to between $797 million and $799 million. Management raised the full-year guide to up to $3.3 billion, up from the prior guide of between $3.265 billion to $3.284 billion.

On the conference call, management noted that they “expect AI Security ARR to exceed $0.5 billion by the end of this fiscal year.” This is a product in which I can see growth rates remaining top tier for a long time, as the rise of AI agents may lead to ever-increasing demand for cybersecurity. In the near term, management noted that the ongoing migration of on-premise SAP software (SAP) to their cloud RISE product is expected to drive solid tailwinds due to customers having the opportunity to add cybersecurity cloud products in the process.

Is ZS Stock A Buy, Sell, Or Hold?

At recent prices, ZS traded at around 12x this year’s sales and 10x 2027e sales. Consensus estimates call for a very modest pace of decelerating top-line growth over the coming years.

The company is expected to deliver solid operating leverage, culminating in around 25% net margins by 2032.

Courtesy of significant weakness in the software sector, ZS is not trading remotely close to bubbly valuations. I expect the company to see top-line growth decelerate more meaningfully than consensus, but even then, the valuation looks more reasonable than in the recent past. Assuming the company can achieve 30% net margins over the long term, that places the stock at around 33x 2027e long-term earnings. Against 15% to 19% revenue growth, that represents around a 2x price-to-earnings growth ratio (‘PEG ratio’), which is reasonable given the company’s high-quality business model. In normal times this would justify a "Buy" rating, but the software sector has significantly underperformed as of late. I’d also note that this valuation multiple looks rather “full” when compared against the mega-cap tech companies like Microsoft (MSFT) or Alphabet (GOOGL) which trade at comparable or lower price-to-earnings multiples.

I am seeing many software names in my coverage universe trading at far lower valuations, even on a growth-adjusted basis. For instance, Salesforce (CRM) trades at around 5x forward sales, with its slower growth rate being offset by high GAAP margins. I find it prudent to remain patient and wait for the stock to trade at around $215 per share, which would represent a 10.5x 2026e and 8.8x 2027e sales multiple.

ZS Stock Conclusion

The market may be too pessimistic about the company’s prospects in the near term, as ZS may see some tailwinds from the RISE migration. That said, the stock still trades at an outsized premium to software peers, and the magnitude of that premium is difficult to ignore. ZS has many of the traits I look for in holdings of the Best of Breed Growth Stocks Portfolio, but strategically speaking, this looks like a buyer’s market in the software sector. I continue to recommend patience in waiting for even more attractive valuations.

data.title: UnitedHealth's 2026 Could Surprise The Street
data.description:
UnitedHealth Group Inc. (UNH) stock is down by over 33% YTD, and I can't say that this massive dip was unjustified. The company's insurance businesses have been facing pressure due to rising medical cost trends, and the medical loss ratio (MLR) has been quite elevated versus the historical norms because of increased senior healthcare utilization. Despite all that, I first called UNH a "Buy" when it was down 43% YTD in mid-May 2025. It looks like I almost caught the local bottom of the price action because, since that call of mine, UNH is up by ~25% (in total returns). And the stock has been rising and beating the market with my every update.

It might look controversial - if the business remains in deterioration, why am I calling the stock a "Buy" then? But I think every dip has its limit, and what matters in such cases is the way the management behaves. I like what I see in UNH's strategic moves lately, and their more conservative approach to drive up the margins. I think that next year, UNH is likely to achieve better-than-expected margins and hence EPS, so that it can restart buybacks and support the stock price. UNH is undervalued in this regard, so I keep a bullish eye on the upcoming 2026.

Why Do I Think So?

My most recent article on UNH came out in August, so I physically couldn't cover the firm's Q3 2025 results. All that is already reflected in the price, but let me briefly take a look at what has happened during the last quarter.

UNH reported ~$113.16 billion in revenues (+12.24% YoY), slightly beating the consensus estimate (I'd call it "in-line reporting"). On the bottom line, UNH's adjusted EPS reached $2.92 (-59% YoY), and while the YoY dynamic looked ugly, the EPS figure itself beat the consensus estimate by ~4%, according to Seeking Alpha. So, effectively, UNH came back to its historical beating potential after two deep misses in Q1 and Q2 2025:

We saw that their medical loss ratio was ~89.9% in Q3 (vs. 85.2% last year), which is exactly the trend that explains why the stock fell so aggressively earlier in 2025. But the management framed the Q3 MLR as "historically high but consistent", likely meaning that Q3 business/industry conditions didn't introduce a new risk to MLR's upside in the future quarters.

For the current year, overall UnitedHealthcare performance remains in line with the expectations we offered in the second quarter. Medical cost trends remained historically high but consistent with our second quarter guidance, and we expect that to continue throughout the remainder of 2025.

Source: CEO's comments on the Q3 earnings call

The new management has been shrinking uneconomic exposure to reverse the margins trend. In particular, now they expect ~1million Medicare Advantage membership contraction in 2026, with a big piece of it coming from plan exits impacting ~600,000 members (plus additional disciplined attrition in group and individual MA). It's a big step toward margin normalization because MA has been a low-single-digit margin business where a small amount of mispricing (or an unusually hot utilization trend) can harm profitability for an entire year. So, by getting rid of those one million members, UNH should effectively cut out the worst-performing cohorts within its sales mix, and so the margins will likely naturally go up throughout 2026 because lower loss-making exposure means better margin-per-member.

I know that the member reduction might seem like a harsh hit to the top-line, but we should remember that UNH is operating with a very large base, having a domestic membership of over 50 million, which was up by 750,000 this year alone (on a YTD basis). And the membership reductions are being paired with explicit pricing resets that support revenue per member:

Moving to ACA markets. We have submitted rate filings in nearly all of the 30 states where we participate that reflect 2025 morbidity and experience. These include average rate increases of over 25%.

Trends for 2025 and our outlook for 2026 remain in line with what we shared in the guidance last quarter. Trends are approximately 11%, and that is how we have priced into 2026, and I'll just share that. With 50% of our January insured business resolved at this point, encouraged by both the yield on persistency and rate to deliver the margin expansion that Tim spoke about in light of that trend of approximately 11%.

Source: UNH's earnings call, the CEO's and CSGO's commentary, emphasis added by the author

So, when the clean-up year ends (I'm talking about FY2026), UNH's top-line is likely to revive because the demographics of an aging population remain a favorable tailwind.

The number of Americans ages 65 and older is projected to increase from 58 million in 2022 to 82 million by 2050 (a 42% increase), and the 65-and-older age group’s share of the total population is projected to rise from 17% to 23%.

Source: pbr.org

The increasing population of older Americans will need health benefit plans and services to manage their healthcare needs. UNH is still the largest publicly traded managed care company by revenue, and it's set to be one of the main beneficiaries of that secular trend. And we see that in FY2027, UNH's revenue growth comes back up above 4% on a YoY basis after a muted FY2026 trend:

What's even more important, all the strategic moves the management makes now to revive margins should translate to a reasonable recovery in EPS already next year, and UNH is also expected to show a 17.33% YoY rise in net earnings in FY2027:

I truly believe that given the company's scale and role in the US market, it should have a valuation premium over its peers. And even compared to historical P/E ratios, we see that on FY2025 EPS, the stock looks undervalued by over 10%.

I'd assume that the management's plan goes the way it's laid out, and the current consensus is overly pessimistic by the average beating size of the past years. In other words, I assume that UNH can beat the current EPS consensus by 7-8% in FY2026. If they beat Q4 2025, and then beat Q1 and Q2 2026 by just a few percentage points, there would come positive revisions, leaving the current FY2026 EPS consensus in the dust. So, my core assumption looks reasonable to me.

Anyway, assuming that UNH comes back up to its 10-year average P/E ratio by the end of 2026, we'd have a price target of $437.70 per share:

17.75 * 1.075 * 22.94x = $437.70/share

This gives me an upside potential of ~30%, and that's why I remain bullish on UNH even after its off-bottom recovery of ~42% so far this year.

Where Can I Be Wrong?

I think that the main risk to my thesis today is if the medical cost trend simply refuses to cool. The Q3 MLR is indeed too high, and it needs to go down by 9-11% just to match the historical norms. So, if the system remains in a senior utilization upcycle, then even a rational plan to revive margins could still lead to choppy earnings ahead. My assumptions for FY2026/2027 EPS growth rates would become too optimistic and misleading, and the stock wouldn't reach the P/E level I'm expecting.

The Bottom Line

Despite the risks, I don't think that UNH actually needs a heroic macro tailwind to show a meaningful recovery from here. The business' stress is visible to everyone, and that elevated MLR has already led to an ugly price action before. In Q3, when the MLR was also red hot, the stock price didn't crash as hard as we've seen before. Yes, it dropped by 16% throughout a few sessions, but instead of continuation, the price recovered, and UNH is down by only 8% since the Q3 print. Assuming that UNH delivers modestly better-than-feared margins as the worst cohorts roll off, I believe the next phase would be upward revisions for EPS figures (for both FY2026 and FY2027). If so, a multiple expansion would follow, and UNH could rise by 30% from here if my valuation model plays out. Based on all that, I remain bullish on UNH stock.

Thank you for reading!

data.title: JCPB: I Prefer JPIE's Risk-Benefit Profile
data.description:
This article updates my review of March 2025 in light of recent performance and current portfolio.

JCPB strategy

JPMorgan Core Plus Bond ETF (JCPB) is an actively managed bond ETF launched on 01/28/2019. JCPB has 2,571 holdings, a 30-day SEC yield of 4.89%, a net yield to maturity of 4.91% and a net expense ratio of 0.38%. Distributions are paid monthly. JPMorgan offers a similar strategy in a mutual fund (HLIPX).

As described in the prospectus by JPMorgan Asset Management, the fund may invest in corporate bonds, U.S. government and agency securities, asset-backed and mortgage-backed securities, unrated and sub-investment grade debt securities, including from international markets. Under normal conditions, the fund enforces maximum limits of:

  • 30% of assets in sub-investment grade debt (“junk bonds”);
  • 10% of assets in sub-prime mortgage-related securities;
  • 25% of assets in international securities.

The fund may also use derivatives for investing and hedging purposes. The portfolio turnover rate was 111% in the most recent fiscal year and 57% in the previous year.

Portfolio

As of writing, the portfolio is mostly invested in securities of U.S. issuers (90.5% of asset value), with significant exposure in treasuries/futures (31.1%), agency mortgage-backed securities (27%) and investment grade corporate bonds (23.6%). No other debt category weighs more than 10%.

Securities with an investment grade (BBB rating or higher) weigh 78.7% of assets, with 47% rated AAA.

The portfolio’s average remaining maturity is 8.35 years. As for interest rate risk, the average duration is 6.08 years.

I won’t list the top 10 holdings here: a single issuer may have many securities in the 2,500+ holdings, and JPMorgan doesn’t provide issuer weights. The sector breakdown suggests that the largest issuers are U.S. Treasury, Fannie Mae, Freddie Mac and Ginnie Mae.

Performance

Since 2/4/2019, JCPB has:

  • outperformed a total bond market benchmark (BND) and a Treasury bond benchmark (GOVT), with similar volatility and a shallower maximum drawdown;
  • almost tied with a corporate investment grade benchmark (LQD), with lower risk in drawdown and volatility;
  • underperformed a high yield bond benchmark (HYG), with lower risk.

The share price has lost 6.4% since inception in January 2019, while cumulative inflation was about 29% (based on CPI).

The annual sum of distributions has increased from $1.66 to $2.38 per share between 2020 and 2024, despite a fall in 2021, as plotted on the next chart. This 4-year dividend growth of 43% is closely related to interest rates trend (second chart below) and should not be projected as a long-term expectation. Nonetheless, it has nicely outpaced inflation.

JCPB vs. competitors

The next table compares characteristics of JCPB and five actively managed bond ETFs with yields close to 5% and at least three years of track record:

  • JPMorgan Income ETF (JPIE).
  • Dimensional Core Fixed Income ETF (DFCF).
  • PIMCO Active Bond Exchange-Traded Fund (BOND).
  • Capital Group Core Plus Income ETF (CGCP).
  • State Street® DoubleLine® Total Return Tactical ETF (TOTL).

This list isn’t intended to be exhaustive.

JCPB is the largest (in assets) and second most liquid (in dollar volume) of these funds, with a middling expense ratio. It comes in second position in total return and is in the middle of the pack for volatility.

JPIE has the highest yield and total return. It also boasts the lowest risk by far based on drawdown and volatility, partly because it has a low interest rate risk (average duration 2.25 years), while its credit risk is slightly higher compared to JCPB (70.7% vs. 78.7% in investment grade).

Takeaway

JPMorgan Core Plus Bond ETF has a 5% yield from an actively managed portfolio of debt securities. JCPB has low credit risk (79% investment grade) and medium interest rate risk (6.08 years duration). It has suffered a moderate decay in asset value since its inception in 2019, but distribution growth has outpaced inflation. JCPB has outperformed several active bond ETFs with similar yields since 2022. However, JPIE, a newer active ETF by JPMorgan with similar fees, looks more compelling based on yield, return, drawdown and volatility.

data.title: Gold Consolidates Near $4,280 As Accumulation Continues
data.description:
By Parshwa Turakhiya

Gold is holding its ground near $4,280 per ounce on Friday, consolidating close to a seven-week high as the broader trend remains firmly constructive. After a strong advance through late summer and early autumn, the metal has absorbed recent pauses without breaking structure, a sign that buyers remain engaged rather than fatigued.

Price behavior near the high points is due to control and patience, not distribution. The daily chart shows a clear sequence of higher lows stretching back to late summer, with gold repeatedly finding support at rising averages. Instead of rolling over after October’s surge, the price consolidated and then resumed its climb. That pattern suggests the market is digesting gains in an orderly way rather than preparing for a reversal.

Rising EMAs keep the trend firmly intact

The EMA structure reinforces the bullish case. Spot gold is trading well above the 20-day EMA near $4,180 and the 50-day EMA around $4,060, both of which continue to slope higher. These averages have acted as reliable support during recent pullbacks, drawing buyers quickly back into the market.

Gold price forecast (Source: TradingView)

Further down the curve, the 100-day EMA near $3,870 remains a key medium-term anchor, while the 200-day EMA around $3,570 highlights the depth of the broader bullish cycle. The distance between price and the longer-term averages underscores how strong the trend has been, but the lack of sharp rejections near recent highs suggests demand remains steady.

Momentum indicators add confirmation. The daily RSI is hovering near $68, elevated but not extreme. This places gold in a strong momentum regime where continuation remains statistically favored. Unlike topping phases where RSI rolls over decisively, momentum here has stabilized and begun to curl higher again, aligning with the recent push toward fresh highs.

Shorter-term price action supports that view. On the 30-minute chart, gold has broken higher from a tight consolidation and is holding above Supertrend support near $4,268. Parabolic SAR remains below price, showing that intraday dips continue to attract buyers. The structure resembles controlled continuation rather than a blow-off, with higher lows forming even as price tests the upper end of its recent range.

Macro tailwinds continue to favor bullion

Macro conditions remain a central pillar of support. Expectations of further U.S. monetary easing have strengthened following fresh signs of labor market cooling. Jobless claims rose more than expected in early December, reinforcing the view that economic momentum is softening and that restrictive policy is no longer warranted.

This comes after the Federal Reserve delivered its third 25-basis point rate cut of the year and signaled a clear shift in tone. Chair Jerome Powell indicated that additional rate hikes are effectively off the table, easing concerns about renewed tightening. Markets have responded by pricing in two rate cuts for 2026, even as the Fed’s own projections remain more cautious.

Liquidity dynamics have also played a role. The Fed’s decision to purchase roughly $40B in short-term Treasury bills to ease money market strains has helped cap short-end yields. That combination of lower rate expectations and added liquidity has compressed real yields, a backdrop that historically benefits gold.

These macro forces have coincided with steady investment demand. ETF flows have remained supportive, indicating institutional participation rather than purely speculative activity. This helps explain why pullbacks have been shallow and why price continues to consolidate near highs instead of retreating sharply.

Key levels define risk and opportunity

From a technical risk perspective, initial support now sits in the $4,240-$4,250 zone, followed by stronger support near the 20-day EMA around $4,180. As long as gold holds above that region on daily closes, the uptrend remains intact. A decisive break below those levels would be the first signal that momentum is deteriorating.

On the upside, a sustained move above $4,300 would likely open the door to another extension higher, with limited historical resistance overhead. The absence of heavy supply near current levels suggests that any breakout could travel quickly if macro conditions remain aligned.

Previously, we discussed how gold’s advance was being driven by a combination of easing expectations, liquidity support, and resilient technical structure. The current consolidation near $4,280 fits that narrative, showing that buyers are comfortable holding exposure even after a strong run.

This material may contain third-party opinions; none of the data and information on this webpage constitutes investment advice according to our Disclaimer. While we adhere to strict Editorial Integrity, this post may contain references to products from our partners.

Original Post

data.title: AI's Impact In Investing: Value Vs. Hype
data.description:
Productivity gains meet process change

Generative AI is moving beyond experimentation and into everyday integration. Broad surveys covering more than 42,000 business leaders show positive return on investment, and asset managers are reporting similar benefits.

For investment teams, the early gains have largely come from efficiency. Quantitative researchers now use large language models (LLMs) to extract data, speed up code development and back-test. Fundamental teams apply the same tools to summarize earnings calls and filings, and assist with first-pass valuation modelling. These changes matter. They have significantly accelerated the research process and reduced time spent on routine tasks.

But because these types of tools are now widely available, they no longer set one manager apart from another. The baseline has moved. In short, these efficiency gains help, but they are not differentiating.

Where AI creates real differentiation

In our opinion, differentiation emerges when AI is used to strengthen human judgment rather than replace it. We see several areas where firms taking this approach are generating real value. Some examples include:

  • Proprietary data pipelines. The advantage here comes not from having AI tools, but from the data behind them and the methods used to turn that data into insight.
  • Creative model application. Some teams are moving beyond standard text models - using network-based signals, synthetic scenario generation, or forward-looking cash flow analytics. These techniques build on existing strengths rather than copying common tools.
  • Always-on awareness. Shifting from point-in-time updates to continuous monitoring gives analysts a more dynamic read on commentary, market reactions, and portfolio signals.

These shifts align with the evolution of Russell Investments’ own manager research process. Our METRiQ tool now auto-generates visuals, tests a strategy’s structural elements, and uses generative AI to identify core strategy characteristics and watchpoints. Importantly, tools like these do not replace human judgment. They help focus attention where it matters - identifying “diamonds in the rough” through machine-learning-based screens and flagging subtle shifts in a manager’s stated edge or portfolio

Distinguishing insight from automation

As AI becomes a standard part of investment workflows, a new challenge is emerging: separating genuine research enhancement from improvements that come only from generic automation. By generic automation, we mean common AI tasks that now come off the shelf - summaries, first-pass analysis, and routine document processing that speed up work but do not create differentiated insight.

There are a few warning signs that show when an AI-driven improvement is not truly differentiating. For instance, crowded signals and alpha decay are appearing in quant strategies that rely on similar text embeddings or sentiment models. Meanwhile, some research teams are beginning to sound more alike as they lean on common LLM interpretations. There is also a risk of overreliance, where repeated use of the same tools contributes to deskilling among research analysts.

At the same time, AI is helping our manager research analysts pinpoint where genuine skill shows up. New analytics now separate stock selection decisions from portfolio construction choices and measure timing effects across industries and factors. By linking these analytics to our deep database of manager records, we can interpret them in the context of each manager’s documented history to pinpoint where value truly comes from. Examples include managers whose security-level return levels matter more than hit rates, or whose disciplined entry-exit decisions support long-term outperformance.

In this sense, AI is not the source of alpha. It is the lens that makes sustainable alpha easier to see.

The path forward

We believe the next phase of AI integration will move from summarization toward situational awareness, supported by systems that track information over time and deliver context rather than content. This reinforces a central belief of ours: AI does not level the playing field but widens it. Managers who combine proprietary inputs, strong governance, and creative application are pulling ahead, while those relying only on common tools risk blending into a crowded middle.

Investor implications

We believe AI will continue to reshape research depth, speed, and structure. The priority for investors is distinguishing true process enhancement from widely available automation. Evaluating data advantage, governance discipline, and integration creativity is becoming as important as assessing traditional investment skill. Ultimately, the real value lies not in the AI tools themselves, but in how managers use them.

Important information pertaining to the hypothetical example: Past performance does not predict future returns. Return level is proportionately scaled in line with cash level to be overlaid. Source: Russell Investments. Assumptions: Average cash level 1.0%, 10-year history from 12/31/2023, gross of fees. Opportunity cost from not securitizing cash varies by asset allocation and time period, and is represented by horizontal bars as marked within the chart legend. Target asset allocation used: 0% cash, 74% MSCI World, 26% Global Aggregate (GBP Hedged). For illustrative purposes only. Does not represent any actual investment. Indexes are unmanaged and cannot be invested in directly. Performance benefit (net) of overlaying cash by last 5 individual calendar year is as follows: 2023:20 bps, 2022:-17bps, 2021:16bps, 2020:14bps, 2019:23bps.

These views are subject to change at any time based upon market or other conditions and are current as of the date at the top of the page. The general information contained in this publication should not be acted upon without obtaining specific legal, tax and investment advice from a licensed professional.

Diversification and strategic asset allocation do not assure a profit or guarantee against loss in declining markets.

Please remember that all investments carry some level of risk, including the potential loss of principal invested. They do not typically grow at an even rate of return and may experience negative growth. As with any type of portfolio structuring, attempting to reduce risk and increase return could, at certain times, unintentionally reduce returns.

data.title: SHLD: Defense Spending, Tech Upgrades And Geopolitics Keep Powering The Rally
data.description:
In August I published my first coverage of the Global X Defense Tech ETF (SHLD). Today, four months later, reading the latest news about Ukraine, the use of frozen Russian assets and the new German defense package, among other news, I find that the thesis I had in mind months ago about SHLD is more valid than ever, because this ETF does not depend on a single catalyst, but on a political, military and technological framework that in my view has become part of the landscape.

And for me, this scenario explains why the fund continues to show such a different momentum from the rest of the ETF universe, which leads me to maintain my buy stance.

A defense market without a roof

When I review the geopolitical dynamics, it is clear to me that last month left us with several clear messages. Ukraine presented a revised peace plan to the United States and Europe, and did so by making it clear that it is not prepared to cede territory. What struck me is that both Zelenskyy and the leaders of E3 (UK, France and Germany) moved in a different direction: focusing instead on ensuring future security, reconstruction and sustained military support. After the meetings, Zelenskyy stated:

Russia is insisting that we give up territories, but we don’t want to cede anything.

To me, that phrase alone sums up why the demand for equipment, defensive technology and advanced systems is not moderating. It is not a one-off demand from Ukraine, it is a joint statement with the most powerful countries in Europe.

And in line with the above, at the same time, the European Union is discussing how to use Russian assets as a financial mechanism for reconstruction, while some countries like Belgium have already marked their objections for legal risks. The discussion is not minor, because if Europe advances with this scheme or decides to finance Ukraine through joint debt, both scenarios imply continuity of military spending and institutional strengthening of the bloc.

For an ETF like SHLD, which combines traditional contractors with specialized software companies, these negotiations translate into something very concrete: more contracts, more cycles of technological update and more long-term visibility.

Germany accelerates and reinforces the trend

The other point I consider key is the announcement of the new €2.9B German defense package, which includes drones, rifles, missiles and the purchase of light helicopters. I consider Germany to be probably the best thermometer of the European strategic turn, because for decades it kept a limited defense profile and today is running multibillion-dollar purchases consecutively.

When a country like Germany increases the scale and speed of its defense spending, the rest of the continent follows suit. And that momentum ends up captured by funds like SHLD which, in addition, have exposure to European manufacturers such as Rheinmetall (RNMBF) and Leonardo (DRS).

Momentum continues to differentiate and liquidity supports the strategy

When analyzing the ETF, I note that the performance of SHLD is not a passing reflection, as in the last 12 months it rose by almost 71%, compared to 9% for the average ETF. In six months I see that it also maintains a notable advantage and, beyond the setback of the last month, I still think that the trend remains aligned with a capital flow that is not disarming.

For me this is not only a "feeling". It is the direct consequence of news such as those I analyzed earlier, where spending is institutionalized, international commitments multiply and open conflicts do not show an immediate resolution.

In terms of liquidity, with almost $5B of AUM and more than $79M traded per day, SHLD moves like a large ETF within a segment that historically did not attract these volumes. That level of liquidity allows me to think of this fund as both a tactical position and a structural bet without worrying about spreads or depth.

The risks are still present but limited

While the context suggests that the ETF has room for further progress, we should not forget to consider risks. And here the most obvious risk I see is political, as decisions on Russian assets, peace plans or even diplomatic tensions may redefine times but hardly change the direction of spending.

I also have to stress that the volatility of the ETF far exceeds the average and its concentration is high, two points which should not be overlooked.

Even so, when I cross these risks with what is happening in Europe, the US and Japan, I find it hard to imagine a scenario where the defense sector returns to pre-conflict levels in the short or medium term.

My final thoughts

After looking at the results of SHLD in the current geopolitical context, I see that the ETF is being consolidated by a momentum above the market, rare liquidity for a thematic fund and a geopolitical thesis that is reinforced with each European decision, each round of negotiations on Russian assets and each equipment package that is approved in Germany.

While I don’t think this is an ETF for ultra-conservative investors, it does seem to me that it’s a powerful tool for those seeking direct exposure to the technological transition and global strategic realignment we are experiencing.

Personally, I still see it as an attractive tactical vehicle within a sector that, far from slowing down, is entering a new stage, so I maintain my Buy stance.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: CURE: The Leveraged ETF That Turbocharges The Leading U.S. Healthcare Stocks
data.description:
Healthcare is one of the leading issues facing the United States in late 2025. The recent government shutdown put healthcare and the Affordable Care Act in the spotlight. The gulf between Democrats and Republicans is vast regarding healthcare, with both sides agreeing to negotiate the future, as several Democrats crossed the aisle to end the shutdown.

Meanwhile, healthcare-related stocks have been rallying, and the Direxion Daily Healthcare Bull 3X Shares ETF (CURE) has exploded higher since the low in early August 2025. If the trend is our friend, healthcare stocks will continue to rally, and CURE could amplify the performance.

CURE has a leveraged long position in pharma and healthcare stocks

The top holdings of the CURE ETF include:

The chart shows that CURE has leveraged exposure to the leading healthcare and pharma companies through swaps and options.

A bullish trend since August

The CURE ETF reached a low of $66 per share on August 7, 2025.

The daily year-to-date chart shows that the CURE ETF rallied 83.5% from the August 7 low to the November 26 high of $121.13 per share. Near the $110 level on December 12, CURE remains in a bullish trend.

The pharma and healthcare ETF rallied due to reduced regulatory uncertainty in the U.S., successful clinical trials and innovation, increasing M&A activity, value-based sector rotation, and the rising prospects for short-term interest rate cuts in 2026.

Approaching a critical technical level

The CURE ETF began trading in June 2011 at just below $5 per share. After reaching a low of $2.95 in August 2011, the trend has been bullish, with CURE making higher lows and higher highs.

The monthly chart shows CURE’s appreciation, leading to a record high of $149.57 in April 2022. The ETF made a lower high at $143.51 in September 2024, which is now the upside target. The recent rally to over $121 in November 2025 suggests that CURE could challenge the first technical resistance level over the coming months.

The reasons to consider a bullish strategy using CURE

The pharma and healthcare ETF rallied due to reduced regulatory uncertainty in the U.S., successful clinical trials and innovation, increasing M&A activity, value-based sector rotation, and the rising prospects for short-term interest rate cuts in 2026. Moreover, the aging U.S. population is increasing healthcare requirements.

CURE has been volatile in 2025, trading from a $66 low to a $121.13 high, but the trend has turned higher since the August low, and the trend is always a trader’s or investor’s best friend. The prospects for the healthcare and pharma sectors remain bullish as the stock market moves toward 2025.

Risk-reward dynamics are critical

CURE is a leveraged ETF, which presents significant risk dynamics. The options and swaps that create the gearing experience time decay, which erodes value. While CURE will amplify the upside performance of its holdings, when they move lower or remain stable, the ETF will lose value.

Leveraged ETFs tend to experience reverse stock splits, but CURE’s history has been the opposite.

Bullish price action in the sector in 2013 and 2015 led to 2:1 and 4:1 stock splits, respectively.

At over $109 per share, CURE had over $179 million in assets under management. CURE trades an average of over 71,600 shares daily and charges a 0.93% management fee.

Given the rally from the August 7 low, CURE’s Seeking Alpha ETF grades earn an A- grade in momentum. The $1.08 blended dividend, which translates to a nearly 1% yield, earns a B+ grade in the dividend category. The assets and daily trading volume earn a B- in liquidity, while the management fee receives a D- grade in expenses. The risks associated with a triple-leveraged ETF receive a failing F grade in the risk category.

I am bullish on the pharma and healthcare sector for the stated reasons, but CURE is a trading product rather than an investment. Any long position in CURE requires a risk-reward plan, including price and time stops to protect capital through avoiding time decay.

When approaching leveraged sector exposure with CURE, time and price stops are appropriate, as the gearing can lead to losses larger than those in the pharma and healthcare stocks. Moreover, stable sector stock prices will likely cause CURE to decline, as time decay erodes its value. The swaps and options that create the triple-leverage dramatically increase risks. The SEC website highlights the significant risks associated with leveraged ETFs, such as CURE.

CURE is a product that amplifies the performance of the healthcare and pharma sector, which could challenge technical resistance over the coming months. Buying CURE on price weakness using price and time stops is a strategy that must include accepting small short-term losses in the quest for greater gains. It is not appropriate to change the price or time stops if CURE does not move in the anticipated direction. However, when the ETF moves higher, adjusting the risk-reward dynamics to protect capital and profits and raising profit targets can lead to successful results.

data.title: Hooker Furnishings: Aggressive Cost Savings Offset Sales Pressure
data.description:
Hooker Furnishings Corporation (HOFT) reported the company’s fiscal Q3 results from the August-October period on the 11th of December. The furnishings company’s performance has remained soft as hospitality shipments have slowed and the furnishings industry is still weak. HOFT has successfully adapted to a weak outlook through an aggressive cost savings program and recent divestments, balancing the earnings outlook.

I remained at a Hold rating in my previous September article on the stock, titled “Hooker Furnishings: Missing A Starting Industry Rebound.” The stock has since returned 4%, nearly on par with the S&P 500’s (SP500) 5% return.

HOFT Q3: Weak Revenues, Offset by Aggressive Cost Savings

Overall, HOFT reported weak Q3 results as the macroeconomic backdrop continues to weigh on the company. After industry-wide furnishings sales showed a starting rebound in early 2025, the furnishings industry’s growth has again stalled to just 1.2% as of September. The housing market has remained very weak due to poor affordability, as I wrote in a recent article, in turn lowering demand for furniture. A weak consumer sentiment and a challenging tariff landscape have only added to the industry’s pressure.

HOFT's Q3 revenues came in at $70.7 million, down by -14.4% year-on-year and missing Wall Street’s consensus by $14.8 million. Hooker Branded even showed positive 1.1% growth for the quarter with a $36.5 million topline, and domestic upholstery followed with 3.0% growth to $30.2 million. On the other hand, the “all other” segment that primarily represents the remainder of Home Meridian showed a massive -76.6% revenue plummet with revenues of only $4.0 million. The lack of large projects within hospitality continues to weigh on HOFT’s total sales performance, while the primary consumer-facing businesses performed largely in line with a weakly growing industry.

The backlog remains weak at $32.7 million after Q3, down by -23.8% year-on-year—the fourth quarter will most likely be weak as well. Branded orders have started to pick up, but as the macroeconomic backdrop remains poor, a near-term recovery shouldn’t be expected.

Aggressive cost cuts have stabilized HOFT’s profitability despite a weak sales performance. HOFT’s gross margin even improved by 80 basis points to 25.6% through strength at Hooker Branded, as the segment’s pricing power was very good. Pricing increases and reduced discounts more than offset cost inflation. On top, HOFT’s aggressive cost savings program, now anticipated to create $25-26.5 million in annual savings by FY2027, reduced overall S&A expenses by $5.9 million in Q3 alone.

Excluding goodwill impairment, HOFT’s operating income came in only slightly negative at -$0.7 million, up by $3.6 million year-on-year. The cost savings program led HOFT to even beat Wall Street's EPS estimates despite the significant revenue miss.

The cost savings program has been critical for HOFT, as earnings would have otherwise plummeted to a concerning level. For now, HOFT seems to be able to navigate industry turmoil relatively well. The focus is on an industry recovery that should also recover HOFT’s earnings level, but with an uncertain housing market outlook, tariff pressure, and a weak consumer, I don’t believe that a major recovery will materialize in the near term.

Home Meridian Subsegment Divestments Improve HOFT's Profitability

HOFT announced in early December that it would be divesting Pulaski Furniture and Samuel Lawrence Furniture for approximately $4.8 million. The brands were previously part of HOFT’s Home Meridian segment. The remaining Home Meridian segment, Samuel Lawrence Hospitality, has been shifted to the “all other” segment following the divestment.

I noted the Home Meridian segment’s struggles in my previous article, and the divestment gets rid of a good part of HOFT’s weakly performing assets. The brands have generated very inconsistent sales and earnings in the past few years, making the divestment accretive to HOFT’s profitability. In Q3, the divested operations’ sales plummeted by -52.3% to approximately $10.3 million. The Home Meridian segment’s margins have been highly concerning.

Considering the financial performance of Pulaski and Samuel Lawrence Furniture, I believe that the divestment is clearly positive for HOFT.

HOFT Stock's Valuation Is Fair

I updated my discounted cash flow (DCF) model for HOFT. I have added $4.8 million in cash from expected divestment proceeds and subtracted the divested segments’ financials from FY2026 forward. The divestment causes quite a sharp decrease in revenues on top of an already weak organic performance.

I assume the furnishing industry to ultimately recover from its current weakness. The timing of a recovery is uncertain for now due to macroeconomic turbulence, but as a base scenario, I estimate revenue growth to be elevated through the FY2027-FY2030 period as industry sales rebound. Afterwards, I assume modest 2% perpetual growth.

As the divestments, HOFT’s aggressive cost savings, and fixed cost leverage on a sales rebound should raise profitability, I estimate the EBIT margin to ultimately recover to 4.5%. The assumption is volatile but, in my opinion, represents a fair base scenario. The after-tax cash flow conversion should remain good.

The estimates put HOFT’s fair value at $11.2, quite near the stock price at the time of writing. I believe that the stock’s valuation fairly reflects current industry turmoil and HOFT’s earnings potential in a normalized macroeconomic backdrop. For now, HOFT still doesn’t seem to have probable upside potential in its valuation.

The fair value estimate is up from $9.2 previously.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital (WACC) of 10.94%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I assume HOFT will pay down its small remaining interest-bearing debt. For the cost of equity, I use the 10-year bond yield of 4.16% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 4.62% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s most recent July estimate for the US. I have kept the beta estimate at 1.36. With a liquidity premium of 0.5%, the cost of equity and WACC both stand at 10.94%.

Takeaway

The industry backdrop has remained challenging for HOFT, and weak hospitality orders have piled on top to create top-line challenges. Positively, HOFT's cost savings and divestments should yield material improvements to earnings—underlying operating income even improved in Q3 despite significant revenue weakness. For now, as the valuation seems balanced, I remain at a Hold rating for HOFT stock.

data.title: The AI Stock Rotation That May Play Out In 2026
data.description:
Artificial intelligence has been a key driver of the recent market rally. While much of the focus has been on semiconductor and power-related equities, Jeff Evans, Vice President, Director, and Lead for Empirical Research and Portfolio Management Support at TD Asset Management, says there could be a strategic shift toward software stocks in the year ahead.

Transcript

Greg Bonnell: Artificial intelligence has been the dominant theme in the recent market rally. But with companies spending billions on new technology, we're also starting to see some questions about the sustainability of this rally. Joining us now to discuss is Jeff Evans from TD Asset Management.

Jeff, great to have you back on the program

Jeff Evans: Great to be here. Thanks for having me on.

Greg Bonnell: I know that TD Asset Management is doing a lot of work on the economics around artificial intelligence. There's huge amounts of capital spending and how it's being funded and whether companies can actually make a return eventually from all this. That's the whole point of this big spend, right? You want to bring money in the door. What are your big takeaways?

Jeff Evans: Sure. So we were always doing deep dives on individual companies that we own in our portfolios, really trying to understand each firm on its own and are they doing things that are logical? What we really wanted to do-- and then we kind of had this exercise last week in one of our team offsites-- we wanted to step back, look at the whole AI infrastructure in total, and just see if the whole group makes sense or if the numbers add up in total.

A couple of interesting things. We started off with the CapEx numbers. So if you look at the hyperscalers-- and that's where the bulk of the spend is on the chips, the data centers, the racking and cooling-- just under $400 billion of CapEx expected in 2025. That ramps up to $600 billion in 2027. And if we'd had this discussion two months ago, at the beginning of October, that was only $500 billion. So we've seen $100 billion increase in CapEx--

Greg Bonnell: Just thrown into the mix.

Jeff Evans: Into the mix. And then the scary thing is, that's per year. So it's going to ramp up to about $600 billion, turns into $3-3.2 trillion of cumulative CapEx by 2030. So that's bigger than Canada-- Canada's a $2.2 trillion economy. So we're talking significant spend from just five or six key companies here.

First question that comes out of that is, how do you pay for that? $3 trillion is not a small sum of money. So where does it come from? The hyperscalers, fortunately, are very profitable companies today. They generate a lot of free cash flow. And if you add that up over the next five or six years, that's about a trillion dollars that they can just recycle of their own capital back into the mix. Still needs the $2 trillion to come up with.

And what you're seeing-- and I think Oracle is kind of setting the tone for this-- is that it'll probably be funded largely with debt. So companies will go out and raise some debt and use that to spend money on chips. And of course, then the question is, do they have the capacity to do that?

Good news is, they have basically no debt today. So you could leverage up to, say, two times EBITDA. That's a perfectly normal number for a large-cap US company. That gives you another $2 trillion of capacity if they do that.

But then you get into an interesting dynamic of, can the debt markets even provide that? Historically, in a good year, the US investment-grade credit market only issues about $150-200 billion of net new debt. Hyperscalers need $400 billion a year. So you're still sitting on a $200 billion deficit. And if the hyperscalers take all of that $150-200 billion--

Greg Bonnell: Yeah, other companies need money.

Jeff Evans: Exactly. They kind of starve everybody else of capital. So there's going to be a very interesting allocation decision here of how much of this actually goes to hyperscalers, how much goes elsewhere. What it could do is, it could push up interest rates and credit spreads up just to net this all out. So that's an interesting piece of this.

The banks will provide some financing. But the other area where you really need investors to come in is through private credit. That's where the big pools of money are. That's where you're probably going to see the bulk of this.

We do think that the money is there, but you're seeing an interesting bumpy ride this year with elevated defaults in private credit. So there's some questions about whether or not they're going to be willing to lend as aggressively, perhaps, as they have in the past. Lots more concern on underwriting, our focus on that today.

So we think this is doable. The $3.2 is probably achievable. You do need a lot of things to go right, though. And there are going to be some interesting decisions along the way on allocating capital back and forth.

So the funding side, we think, is good. Then the other big question, of course, is, can you earn a return on this? We went through an exercise where we-- it was sort of an unfortunate exercise, but we went through all the white-collar jobs that AI could potentially replace. That's somewhere between a $7 to $10 trillion wage pool globally. If you assume about a 10% share that AI captures by 2030, that's $700 billion of revenue. Obviously, they have expenses to run their businesses. So you're probably looking at $300 billion of profit on $3 trillion of CapEx. That's a 10% return.

So we came out of this pretty comfortable that you could earn a return. But the interesting question is, is 10% good enough? The hyperscalers make a lot of money. They're 20% to 30% return-on-equity businesses. 10% may not be enough to keep investors happy in that.

So, our key takeaway here is there's probably some more work for investors to do on the financing side to really make sure that this gets financed. And then it looks like we can earn a return on this over time. It's still too early to know exactly what that's going to be. And there is a risk that this could be a little bit weaker than what investors have come to be accustomed for in the tech stocks.

Greg Bonnell: I understand as you're doing this research, you actually said if we're going to research AI, why don't we use AI to do some of this and identify some major themes. Semis, grid infrastructure, and software, I think, is what it came up with.

Jeff Evans: Exactly. So the big challenge is there's AI, but then the reality is there's lots of little themes that are going on, lots of rotations under the surface-- the chip companies, the hyperscalers, the software, the grid, all of these different pieces. And so we used AI to help us classify all of these companies. We said, just put them into different buckets. You can only be in one bucket at a time.

And then what we did is, we said is there momentum? Is there thematic rotation or momentum across these different themes? And what we found was that about a 9- to 12-month horizon. If one of these themes get going, if the market gets behind it, it tends to stick around for up to about a year. So that was interesting to see.

And then it's also been interesting is how that's ebbed and flowed over the course of the year. If you go back to January, it was all about the chip companies, NVIDIAs and Broadcom. We've seen that roll off a little bit. They're still very strong overall, but not in our top five theme buckets now.

And what has been a little bit more persistent is the power side. It's actually getting the power to these data centers, the transformers, the power lines, the natural gas generators, and turbines. All of that has been fairly persistent and stayed within the top end of the bucket. And then what we're seeing now more recently is, it's the software companies. It's the end-use cases that the market is starting to focus on. So a bit of a migration away from the chips more to the end-use cases is what we've seen evolve over the course of the year.

Greg Bonnell: At any point when you're asking AI about AI, was it critical of itself?

Jeff Evans: It was an interesting process. We did lots of iteration. And there's a view that you can type this in, get an answer back in 20 minutes. I think we spent about five days in total working with AI to actually come up with this project. So, it was not as straightforward as one might initially think, but it led to some pretty interesting results.

Greg Bonnell: A lot of fears about bubbles. Obviously, the bubble chatter seems to pop up all through the year, right? And then people start saying, is this 1999 all over again with the internet boom and bust? The internet's still here. So you look at valuations. You look at earnings growth. And we hear about the bubble chatter. Does it all make sense?

Jeff Evans: So part of that was this exercise of, is this actually going to make sense in the long run on the AI? And again, we're comfortable you can earn a return. That was very different in 2000. There's lots of fiber optic cable that got put in the ground that is only now earning a return a couple of decades later.

So we're more optimistic that these companies can earn a return faster on that. But if you look at the broader equity market, the S&P's not trading at 40 times earnings the way it was in the dotcom bubble, but it is at 22. That is the highest outside of the bubble it's ever traded at.

Earnings growth expectations are about 15% per year for the next two years. That's three times higher than average. So these are some pretty lofty earnings growth expectations. As long as we get that, the multiples are fine. The bigger question is, are we going to see that?

What we have seen most recently is companies are meeting and exceeding those estimates. In the third quarter, companies blew through estimates and raised guidance pretty much across the board. So the short-term data looks optimistic. I think what investors need to focus on in 2026 is if companies are showing any signs of not being able to meet these very elevated expectations, that's where it becomes concerning.

But then you look at all the other macro. We're getting interest rate cuts. We're getting the OBVA stimulus kicking in next year. If it's going to happen, it's probably more of a back-half thing, but certainly something for investors to keep a focus on - are companies meeting very, very elevated earnings growth expectations?

Original Post
